data.title: Canadian Natural Resources: Earnings Set To Reveal Massive Rewards
data.description:
Oil prices have been all over the headlines for most investors around the world because oil prices impact everything else. If we think about it, oil is largely foundational for much in our modern society. From plastics, gasoline, and lubrication to everything in between. Oil plays a pivotal role, whether it's supplying the energy and power, the heat, or the base feedstock for many different items.

So when oil prices see massive levels of volatility, the question many investors ask themselves is, how do we benefit from this environment? Especially if your wallet is going to get hurt in so many other avenues of life, how can we benefit from the oil volatility in your portfolio?

Today and tomorrow, I plan on covering two outstanding commodity-focused investments that benefit from this increased oil pricing volatility. When it all settles down, the dust is settled, the Iran war is over, and oil prices come back down to where they were previously, these companies are set to reward their shareholders massively, unlike many of their peers.

Let's dive in!

Northern Oil Producer That's Safe With A Low Breakeven Price

Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) is primarily a Canadian-based and Canadian-focused oil and natural gas producer.

CNQ does have assets in Africa and Europe, but the lion's share of their revenue is generated by their Canadian assets.

Being a Canadian O&G producer has not been easy for most of 2026. Originally, oil prices were expected to be trending downward this year. When we covered CNQ in December, we wrote.

The other primary risk to CNQ is geopolitical. As with any oil and gas producer, the price of oil can heavily impact their earnings. Oil prices are expected to be lower in 2026 than they were in 2025. Even though demand is rising, there is a greater supply in the market, and that supply has to be absorbed before prices are expected to recover. CNQ uses West Texas Intermediate, WTI, crude oil prices to measure its break-even price. The break-even price is the price where CNQ turns a profit on its oil production. According to CNQ, in March of 2025, their break-even price was low to mid $40 per barrel. Right now, it's expected for WTI to be around the $51-52 range per barrel in 2026. This leaves them room for their prices to fall further and CNQ to still be operating at a profit. Growth for CNQ will need to focus on a lower break-even point for them to find added profitability throughout next year.

CNQ struggled heavily due to the United States military operation in Venezuela. This led to a strong sell-off to start 2026.

Oil prices in the United States dropped in the belief that cheap heavy oil from Venezuela was going to negatively impact oil prices by adding more supply to a market awash with oil. We took time to review the impact of Venezuelan oil on the overall oil supply market and determined that, in the short term, it wasn't going to be a big issue for CNQ. We wrote a review of CNQ in light of the new potential oil paradigm.

CNQ estimated that they are able to break even if oil prices within the United States were to fall to $40 a barrel. At this point in time, prices are still well above that and are not expected to fall below $40 a barrel in 2026, even factoring in Venezuelan crude. CNQ remains an attractive investment and is worth adding to. This unexpected geopolitical event only drives it further into deep value territory. Many are pre-selling on the fear of oil production that hasn't occurred and won't be able to occur in the near-term.

As many of you know, without needing to be reminded, there was a lot of panic. There was also a new brewing situation. It wasn't many months later that the United States engaged in warfare with Iran. This quickly led to the Strait of Hormuz closing, preventing 25% of global seaborne oil from safely passing through a pivotal chokepoint. Oil prices rebounded and even hit triple digits, over double what many predicted oil would be trading for in 2026 because of concerns of oversupply. Now there was a concern of a lack of supply.

Oil prices are significantly higher than where they were at the beginning of the year and well above the forecasted price of West Texas Intermediate crude being $52 a barrel in 2026, as we mentioned in December of 2025. CNQ has a breakeven price in the $40 range, meaning that any dollar that oil trades above that is profitable for them, and the higher it goes, the bigger their profit goes.

CNQ has a relatively balanced approach to its overall production, with a strong level of production coming from oil and a strong level of production coming from natural gas. Natural gas prices really haven't been heavily impacted by the recent military action, but to put into perspective how much revenue CNQ generates from each of those businesses, we can understand why oil prices have a greater impact on them.

CNQ generated $19 billion CAD in Oil and Natural Gas Liquids (NGL) revenue compared to $2 billion CAD in Natural Gas. Oil and NGLs are clearly the major financial drivers of CNQ.

Clearly, the market initially understood the benefit of higher oil prices to CNQ, and investors drove its price upward strongly along with many other major oil and natural gas producers.

Why is CNQ positioned better than most of them to reward shareholders from this temporary oil price situation? It all comes down to how CNQ is focused on rewarding shareholders. CNQ has a structure that, when its net debt falls below a certain amount, more and more of its cash flow is directed towards shareholders. In their last earnings release, they adjusted that ratio to be more favorable towards common shareholders.

According to management, their net debt sat at approximately $16 billion at the end of 2025. They were able to raise their dividend to common shareholders in March of this year to further reward shareholders. Free cash flow is returned to shareholders in the form of share buybacks and debt reduction on top of the regularly growing common dividend that CNQ has been providing shareholders.

This structure allows them to reward shareholders rapidly when excess cash flow is produced. To understand the extent to which this would be beneficial to shareholders, we must look at the sensitivity of their free cash flow. Thankfully, CNQ provides a clear outline of how sensitive free cash flow would be versus various oil pricing situations.

If WTI is priced near $85 a barrel for an extended period of time, its free cash flow is 91% higher than if it were at $65. Currently, as we've seen above, WTI is priced in the $90-a-barrel range, meaning there is nearly a 100% benefit to their free cash flow. This excess cash flow would be distributed to shareholders on a prorated basis. Considering that net debt sat near $16 billion, we are right on the threshold of 60% and 75% levels of this additional cash flow going directly to shareholders.

Furthermore, operational cash flow expectations for CNQ have been rising as the year goes on and oil prices remain elevated. This cash flow is the lifeblood of the company, and as that expectation rises, so does the fair value expectation we have for CNQ over the long term.

The question will be how CNQ chooses to predominantly reward shareholders with this excess cash flow?

  • If they continue to cut down on their net debt, we would see rewards spread over the long run.
  • If they decide to do buybacks, they are limited to how much they are allowed to buy back as a Canadian company. Currently, CNQ is authorized to buy back up to 10% of its public float from March 2026 to March 2027. This would require $8.3 billion at current prices, equivalent to all of their shareholder returns in 2025 (inclusive of common dividends, share buybacks, and excess debt retirement). Ironically, if WTI prices remain elevated, they'd potentially have the excess cash flow to nearly do so.
  • If they decide to do a special dividend, we'd see a one-time bonus cash payout and likely an extremely elevated share price for a limited time.

In all scenarios, common shareholders see strong benefits immediately. Net-debt reduction would allow the benefits to be doled out over a longer, more level period of time, likely boosting the expected total return of CNQ over the long run. A rapid buyback of shares would cause a strong price increase in their trading values and a reevaluation of their fair value from all per-share values growing rapidly as shares are retired. A special dividend is always a welcome one-time reward from an unusual oil pricing situation.

In the end, my personal preference would be to see an increase in buybacks and debt retirement versus a one-time special dividend. Retirement of debt and shares produces a more sustainable long-term ability for them to grow their dividend and to reward shareholders, versus a one-time large payout. In the event of a one-time large payout, we would likely see a large price swing as investors buy shares simply to try to collect the payout and then sell out afterward. Either way, earning forecasts for CNQ are likely muted compared to the reality of the benefit they will receive from higher oil prices overall because of the Iran War.

The CNQ releases its next earnings during the pre-market hours of May 5. Oil prices started to climb heavily after the close of the first quarter this year, meaning that this quarter's earnings are not likely to be extremely spectacular. I expect a focus on the events that have occurred in the time frame thereafter and the rewards that CNQ has planned for its shareholders. This is something most investors are not considering: the fact that CNQ is primed to reward them heavily from their increased cash flow from higher oil prices. The longer oil prices remain elevated, the greater the basket available for rewarding shareholders becomes. Once oil prices return to prior levels, CNQ continues to have strong operations and a low breakeven price. This allows them to continue to be a spectacular long-term investment.

Conclusion

CNQ falls into a rare group of oil and gas producers that have focused on shareholder returns more than on growing production. If we consider their low break-even point per barrel of oil for their operations, we can understand that as a very insulated oil and gas company, meaning that there isn't great regional risk to their production because they are based in Canada, we're able to benefit from this increased volatility of oil prices for as long as it lasts. The longer that oil prices remain elevated because of the conflict in Iran, the greater the amount of rewards in free cash flow that CNQ generates, and when they have more free cash flow, shareholders have more rewards in the long run.

Since our initial coverage of them back in December, CNQ has returned over 40% as total return compared to the market's muted 4.6% returns. We expect that CNQ may not have that much gas left in the tank for the share price unless there is phenomenal growth developed by the excess free cash flow rewards. This is why I think it's a great time to "Buy" now, before earnings, because when everyone hears of the rewards that they have available to shareholders, they'll start crowding in.

It's this kind of clarity above the chaos of day-to-day life that we provide to Dividend King members via data-driven, facts-first fundamental analysis.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Commodity Idea investment competition, which runs through May 31. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Everus Construction Group: Skilled Labor Shortage Will Drive This Market Leader Higher
data.description:
Everus Construction Group (ECG) is a specialty construction contractor, recently separated from a general contractor by the name of MDU Construction Services Group.

Everus operates within two critical segments:

  • Electrical & Mechanical: Includes construction and maintenance of electrical and communication wiring, as well as mechanical piping and services.
  • Transmission & Distribution: Includes services for private and public companies focusing on generation and distribution of electricity, which covers overhead and underground electric power.

Since the date of the spinoff, Everus has seen a tremendous expansion in its E&M segment, which now represents a staggering 88% of backlog due to the expansion related to the data center infrastructure buildout.

I strongly believe that Everus Construction Group is a market leader for multiple reasons, as this article will highlight. These reasons include:

  1. A shortage in skilled electrical labor
  2. Acquisition tailwinds
  3. Relative valuation

Based on the above reasons, I would like to issue a "Strong Buy" rating on ECG, even after some potential risks around execution.

General Outlook on the AI Supercycle

Starting off, I would like to mention that it is important to recognize that the AI industry is cyclical. This extends to every part of the industry that benefits from increased capital expenditures, which extends to not only the Nvidias and Taiwan Semiconductors of the world, but also to more specialized companies such as Everus. However, it is also important to understand the size and timing of this cycle.

Coatue Capital Management depicts the following visualization:

It is an investor's job to understand where we are at in the cycle of AI.

Currently, we are seeing that demand is not only accelerating but also increasing in duration. For example, companies like Micron (MU), a key supplier of memory chips (a core component of any data center), have signed their first five-year Strategic Customer Agreement. Agreements like these speak volumes to the demand and commitment from the industry to continue the expansion of the AI buildout, specifically within memory, which has typically relied on short term SCAs, such as 1 year or shorter.

Going away from memory and going closer to an industry more related to the business of Everus, I would like to discuss the shortage within electrical equipment.

As reported by the Financial Post, the US is facing a major shortage of electrical equipment, specifically transformers and switchgears. This is forcing companies to rely on foreign-made equipment, often of Chinese make.

This is far from ideal, as importing equipment usually adds to timelines, can create supply chain risks, and is often more expensive due to shipping costs. However, this is the situation that these data center operators are currently finding themselves in. They are forced to take on these risks just to get ahead of their neighbor, who is also adding megawatts of data center capacity next door.

The above two shortages are not the first and not the last. To me, it is very apparent that we are nowhere near the top of this cycle and that the inflection point of a deceleration in demand is not on the horizon.

With this information, an investor can, in fact, say that this cycle is indeed different.

By establishing the above, I can now open myself to discuss and invest in a name like Everus, which to me relies on the very above shortages. This is not only due to the fact that they directly support the case for continued acceleration in data center buildout, but also because continued acceleration is bound to continue or even exacerbate investor sentiment within this segment.

Skilled Labor Is Becoming a Key Bottleneck In AI Buildout

According to many sources, which I will discuss below, shortages within the AI expansion are branching out from only the sectors that we tend to hear about daily. As demand picks up more and more, more and more sectors appear with significant pricing power due to shortages, including sectors such as memory, advanced packaging, and electrical equipment. In my job as an analyst, I aim to find the next bottleneck. To me, one of these bottlenecks that we may encounter is around skilled labor. Specifically, I will be discussing electrician labor shortages.

Looking at the construction of a typical data center, sources indicate that skilled electrical labor accounts for roughly 45% to 70% of total data center construction costs. There is no debate to consider this a key step within the entire life cycle of a data center.

To be able to keep up the current momentum of data center construction in the US, Associated Builders and Contractors, a trade organization, estimates that the US economy will require an additional net 349,000 new workers in 2026, followed by an additional 456,000 new workers. The main driver in this strong growth is the resurgence in construction spending, driven by the concentrated focus of data center expansion.

The same report goes on to say the following:

Demand for electricians capable of precision wiring has surged due to the rapid increase in data center construction. Recent industry efforts to accelerate skilled worker development have helped, but the industry is effectively swimming upstream. Approximately one-fifth of all electricians are over 55. Worker shortages also remain more severe in areas associated with industrial megaprojects, including semiconductor fabrication facilities.

On top of the above information, Brookings indicates that one large data center project can absorb as many as 1,500 skilled trade workers, meaning that every new data center announcement puts more pressure on an industry that is already constrained with crackdowns on immigration by the Trump administration. After all, as the same report indicates, roughly 30% of skilled trade workers in the US are immigrants, with some being undocumented.

As we all know, AI data centers will put tremendous strain on the grid infrastructure of the US. However, it is important to consider the scale and growth rate of this trend. Looking at the information we have presented today, the growth is truly unprecedented.

McKinsey indicates that electricity demand for data centers will grow to 606 TWh by 2030, from a current base of just under 300 TWh. This is expected to represent nearly 12% of US power demand.

Why Everus Construction Group?

Thus, taking the above information into account, I believe that there is significant merit to considering a data center and grid expansion-focused construction services company as a potential addition to the portfolio.

When considering a company within such a sector, investors need to clearly understand what they are investing in. Although I believe that Everus is a significant beneficiary of the above trends, as will be described below, it is also important to understand that by design, the moat of a company such as Everus is actually quite small. After all, Everus and their competitors do not have a significant technology moat. This is the reason for the low margins in the sector.

That said, that's not necessarily a bad thing. After all, the shortages mentioned above actually significantly strengthen the moat of the company (by preventing new companies from entering). In effect, this serves as a tailwind for multiple expansions as investors price in a business that possesses a resource that is difficult to access, resulting in a barrier to entry.

Thus, to be able to participate in this trend, I look for the following:

  1. Pure play on the trend I am looking to capture
  2. Strong execution and visibility into future revenue
  3. Experience within the sector

Everus possesses all 3 traits, in my opinion.

It is a pure-play in the sense that out of all competitors, it is the most exposed to data centers, high-tech (semiconductor fabrication, etc.), and grid expansion. While most companies do not break out backlog per end market, Everus stated the following on their last earnings call:

Our record backlog really provides us a clear line of sight for 2026, and some of those projects go into 2027. And if you look at where those backlog contributions are coming from sequentially, it's not just data centers. It is largely data centers, but it's also hospitality and high-tech, it's substation and transmission as well. - Jeff Thiede, Everus CEO

Additionally, the below can be seen from the company's latest earnings presentation, where we can see that data centers represent up to 55% of TTM revenues.

When looking at strong execution, this is evident in many aspects with Everus. In 2025, the company raised full-year guidance two times - in Q2 and Q3 2025. Backlog has expanded significantly, with approximately 16% growth, despite record execution in 2025.

Additionally, margins have expanded across the board, with both net income and EBITDA margins for the year increasing by 40 bps. While this may seem trivial, due to the low margin nature of the sector, this represents a significant uplift to earnings.

Lastly, I look for a company that has a strong track record to their name. It is important to mention that although Everus was only trading on the stock market for just over a year and a half, it was spun off from a company by the name of MDU Construction Services Group, which has publicly traded since 1996.

Competition Is Too Diversified

Of course, other competition exists and must be mentioned. The largest player in the sector is Quanta Services (PWR), a construction services provider with a market cap roughly 15 times larger, at ~$95B, as of April 24th, 2026.

Although it seems that PWR has been executing extremely well, this company is too diversified for me to confidently say that it captures my thesis well. From the company's 2026 investors day presentation:

As can be seen above, Quanta is one of the main players within the expansion of the US grid system, with the Electric Grid & Gas Utility segment making up around half of 2025 revenue. While this does play into my thesis, the Technology & Load Center segment, which includes data centers, is only 10% of revenue.

While it is no doubt a high-quality company, it does not suit the thesis as well as Everus.

Another player to include is MYR Group (MYRG), which is also highly focused on T&D (Transmission & Distribution), while also being significantly more diversified between not only data centers but also healthcare, airport projects, transportation, clean energy, and EV charging.

Thus, it seems that Everus is as close to a pure play on data center construction as one can find.

Discounted Valuation

Comparing typical metrics, such as GAAP Price/Earnings over time, shows that Everus is by far the cheapest, especially when compared to a giant, such as PWR.

Despite this, analyst estimates, as presented by Seeking Alpha, indicate that Everus is the market leader in revenue growth and close to par with other players.

Ticker Revenue Growth (FWD) Diluted Net Income Growth (FWD)
ECG 17.64% 17.90%
PWR 16.70% 19.70%
MYRG 9.6% 23.8%

It is also important to consider that Everus has handily beat analyst estimates in previous quarters, with EPS on average 39% above estimates in the last 4 quarters, as per Seeking Alpha.

With the above considered, this also doesn't mean that Everus will necessarily re-rate to similar valuation levels as peers. Peers deserve a premium over Everus due to diversification in both end market and geography, as well as scale (all of which adds to dependability of cash flows).

However, this does mean that Everus is still a strong opportunity to participate in a pure-play data center construction company, with a strong track record of execution. As Everus continues to execute, it can be expected that valuation will re-rate to some extent with the growth of the company.

Acquisition of SE&M

In line with the above thesis of valuation expansion, Everus has acquired a sizeable construction company operating in the Southeast United States.

The construction industry has historically been very dependent on M&A in order to expand, and this transaction is no different.

That said, it is important to note that this acquisition will allow Everus to significantly expand into the Southeast United States, an area that is currently underrepresented by Everus, and one that management has stated they want to expand into if the right opportunity presents itself.

If you look at where our footprint is, there's opportunities across the country, particularly as you kind of get down to the South and Southeast. I'm not saying that that's where we're headed, but those are opportunities if we find the right work and the right set of jobs to expand on there. - Maximillian Marcy, Everus CFO

The acquisition cost the company $158M, which is expected to be funded by cash on hand.

In terms of revenue, SE&M delivered $109M of revenue in 2025, with EBITDA margins "in the high teens". Running a quick back-of-the-envelope calculation and assuming 17.5% EBITDA margins, this would put SE&M at around $19.1 TTM EBITDA, meaning that the acquisition was around 8.3x EV/EBITDA.

In the previous earnings call, management mentioned that typical multiples for M&A in recent times are around 9-10x, meaning that this deal came at a discount, while also expanding Everus's presence in a historically underdeveloped region.

Investors should rejoice, as it seems that Everus found the deal they were looking for.

Risk

No thesis is risk free.

The main risks to me emerge around execution. Specifically, I would like to mention margins.

In an industry such as construction, margins are incredibly tight. This means that even a small deterioration can have a tremendous impact on valuation and, as such, share price.

I recommend investors monitor net income and EBITDA margins, especially as Everus aims to integrate a sizeable, $158M company into its operations. Challenges within this space may push me to think about competition as alternative investments.

Conclusion

Taken together, Everus Construction Group presents an excellent investment opportunity.

As the AI industry seems to be expanding at full throttle, news of new bottlenecks within the space appears to be popping up seemingly every other week, and Everus is well positioned to capture the next bottleneck, being skilled electrical labor.

As compared to the competition, Everus presents itself as a pure play on data center construction, and offers an excellent track record of execution, with a strong backlog and solid inorganic growth opportunities on the horizon.

Thus, I am very bullish on the construction services sector, where I see specifically Everus Construction Group come out as a market leader in 2026.

I would like to initiate my rating of ECG with a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: NCR Voyix: More Than Promises Needed
data.description:
NCR Voyix (VYX) has long been a software company that does not have the software growth record. Management had previously gotten out of a lot of other businesses, as I had noted. But my last article warned investors to wait for solid progress before considering this idea. The stock price has continued to decline considerably since that was written. So, I will remain with a strong sell idea until I see solid progress. For me, there is far too much chance of a loss of investment principal for me to consider this idea.

Stock Price

The stock price action alone indicates that considerable caution is needed when reviewing an investment idea like this.

The long-term stock price chart action alone is a huge warning about the situation. Whenever an investor sees something like this, it is likely akin to stepping in front of a "runaway train" rather than contrarian investing. The further risk of loss of principal is huge. Even if the investor were to invest in the stock after a (for example) 50% rally (assuming that things are materially better), there likely would be a long way (still) back to where this stock price should be.

This is clearly a company where management has been long on promises of better times and short on actually producing those better times. Whatever is going on here has to be a major turnaround situation that is likely to take a while. Hence the advice to wait for solid evidence that a turnaround is under way.

The price-earnings ratio shown above is clearly in the contrarian investment strategy range. But before that is ever a consideration, the whole story on the corresponding stock price action needs to be extremely well understood. Clearly something has been going on for a very long time that needs solid proof of a resolution before anyone even thinks about this.

Management

One has to remember that management is the biggest asset or liability not on the balance sheet. Even if there has been a strategy change to make up for what is going on with the stock price, there needs to be solid evidence of a stock price turnaround.

Generally, that means that management has to be very blunt about the situation and what they are going to do about it. In fact, I regularly look for that bluntness in any quarterly review (or annual meeting). Managements that are realistic in a very upfront way about what is going on are highly likely to resolve the issue.

When that realism becomes apparent, rarely, if ever, does the stock price action shown above result. Since my last article, the stock price is down nearly 40%. It would therefore take close to a (roughly) 70% stock price rise just to get back to where the stock price was when the last article was written.

Once you realize that quick figuring, you then realize there is plenty of time to evaluate this situation from the sidelines before deciding to "jump in".

Combine that with the fact that larger companies take more time to improve the situation than smaller companies. So, there is no rush here when it comes to missing an opportunity.

What I Am Looking For

The first quarter report will happen on May 7. From what I can tell, there need to be some major changes in both the results and the strategy success going forward.

Growth

This is essentially a software company now. Software companies generally grow quickly, or there is no reason to consider them. There is nearly always someone or a company that has a solid growth idea at a reasonable price when it comes to software.

To me that means that recurring revenue needs to grow at least 10% annually (and preferably 15%). Companies that are not growing in the software industry are generally a big investment red flag.

As shown above, recurring revenue comparison is nothing close to what is needed. That is going to limit the long-term improvement of EBITDA and profits. Furthermore, the company reported a loss in fiscal year 2024 using the GAAP numbers. Personally, I want to see a few years of good, solid GAAP returns, probably in excess of 15%, whenever I consider this type of investment. For me, the earnings of the fiscal year after the reported loss "start the clock". I can be very patient when a loss is involved because patience is often worth a lot more than money.

Cash & Debt Summary

This appears to be a situation that can be handled. The debt is not due for a bit, although refinancing initiatives typically start about two years ahead of time.

The thing to remember is that free cash flow is non-GAAP. Therefore, companies are free to present whatever they want as free cash flow as long as they explain it here and in the filings.

In this case, the company does go the "extra mile" to detail that much of the free cash flow was used for "strategic initiatives" like restructuring. To me that should mean that stock repurchases are "off the table" until that debt is at a manageable level, given the need to use free cash flow elsewhere.

I have always been of the opinion that software in general advances quickly enough that debt is not advisable. I would therefore like to see that debt repaid before any shares are repurchased. The company may need the financial flexibility in the future.

Summary

I personally would like to see 5 years of solid improvement from the current situation before I would consider this investment. Because I prefer to watch for that improvement from the sidelines, I rate this a strong sell until further notice.

I have been well aware of this company for some time, as I first began following it when it got into the ATM business back in the 1980s to compete with Diebold (DBD) back then and IBM (IBM). Of course, those days are long gone. It is one of many forays that this company has tried and later abandoned.

The issue here is the constant foray into new ideas that just never stuck. Benefits may have happened for a time, but it would appear that those benefits were never permanent (enough).

Risks

The biggest risk is that the company and the stock price will "turn the corner". But then again, this stock price is down so far, that one can give up an initial move because there will be plenty left long-term for any investor. I view the current situation as much too dangerous because it involves a large chance of principal loss. I think there are better risk-reward scenarios out there that offer a far better risk-adjusted return.

Investment is all about repeating results consistently. That makes this idea an idea for a very advanced investor that knows how to handle the situation (whether up or down). This is not for the average investor.

To me, any software company needs to have very minimal or no debt on the balance sheet at all times. That part of any industry moves way too fast for debt to be an option.

data.title: VanEck Mid-April 2026 Bitcoin ChainCheck
data.description:
Two historically bullish signals are flashing on bitcoin: negative funding rates and a clustered hash rate drawdown, as volatility cools.

Weekly Bitcoin ETP Flows (USD)

Following 5 consecutive weeks of outflows from 1/24 through 2/21 totaling roughly -$4B, spot bitcoin ETP (BTC-USD) flows reversed in late February and have been net positive in 6 of the last 7 weeks through 4/11.

Funding Rates Turn Negative: A Contrarian Buy Signal

Bitcoin price action was energetic over the past 30 days, with several 20% drawdowns and rallies amid the US-Iran conflict. As a ceasefire materialized, BTC realized volatility tumbled from a period high of ~56% to settle around ~41%. Low sentiment on bitcoin pressured the 30-day moving average (MA) annualized funding rate (FR) to 2.1%, down from 2.7% 30 days earlier. The funding rate now stands at the 10th percentile since November 2020. On a shorter timeframe, the 7-day MA bitcoin funding rate turned negative, hitting its lowest levels since 2023 at (-1.8%).

Examining instances since 2020 where 7-day bitcoin funding rates turned negative, there is a substantial uplift in average returns as well as a higher probability of positive returns across 30-day, 60-day, 90-day, and 180-day horizons. The mean return uplift for negative FR periods was +630 basis points (bps), and the data show this uplift scales inversely with the depth of negative funding.

For context, the mean 30-day BTC return since 2020 is +4.5%, while the mean 30-day return for negative FR periods was +11.5% with a 77% hit rate. When bitcoin funding dropped below -5% annualized, BTC showed a +19.4% return (+1,400 bps uplift) on 30-day periods and a +70% return (+2,900 bps uplift) on 180-day horizons. Negative FR days produced 19 of the top 50 180-day return periods since 2020, despite occurring only 13.6% of the time. Five of the top 10 single-day BTC returns occurred after purchasing during negative funding periods, as did 10 of the top 20.

Options Positioning: Hedging at Peak Bearishness

Put premiums reached all-time highs over the last 30 days, reflecting a peak in hedging and bearish positioning. On a relative basis, put premiums paid as a share of BTC spot volume surged +120% year-over-year to reach 10 bps and were up +21% versus the prior 30-day period. On an absolute basis, the 7-day MA for put premiums paid is up +19% month-over-month but has come down -71% since its peak on 3/30. While peak absolute bearishness may have passed, investors remain meaningfully bearish on BTC in relative terms.

Put Premiums to Spot Volume More Than 6x Higher Than April 2024

Onchain Activity and Long-Term Holder Behavior

Bitcoin network onchain activity was generally lower over the past 30 days. Daily transactions surged +22% month-over-month to 545k, reaching the 96th percentile all-time. Despite that jump in volume, daily active addresses slipped -3% month-over-month (51st percentile), while new addresses were down -2% month-over-month. Transfer volume recorded $48.5B/day (81st percentile), down -5% month-over-month as positioning flux dropped alongside volatility declines.

The share of active supply in the last 180 days dipped -160 bps to 28.4% (34th percentile), suggesting an increasing tendency toward holder dormancy. Average daily fees dropped -5% month-over-month to ~$169k (49thpercentile) and are down -66% year-over-year, while mean transaction fees fell -22% month-over-month to $0.31 (48thpercentile), well below the $1.27 level from a year ago.

Spent Volume Ticked Up for All Long-Term Holders in April m/m

Note: Many assume “spent volumes” are a good proxy for BTC sales. However, not all long-dormant coin movements represent selling. Some transfers are sent to quantum-resistant addresses, others are contributions to digital asset treasuries (DATs), and some reflect routine wallet maintenance.

The most recent 30-day period shows a broad rebound in spent volume across all holding cohorts. The younger end of the long-term holders (1y-2y, 2y-3y, 3y-5y) sent more bitcoin, up +47%, +52%, and +26% month-over-month, respectively, but their activity was -22%, -24%, and -71% below their 12-month averages. Over the past 4 weeks, the 3y-5y group sent the second-smallest amount of bitcoin since December 2023. As spikes in transfer volumes in the 3y-5y group tend to coincide with 4-year cycle traders, it is logical to see these figures pare back as prices wind down and the cycle resets.

Longer-term supply holders increased transfer activity over the last 30 days. The 5y-7y, 7y-10y, and 10y+ segments sent 72k, 45k, and 18k BTC in the trailing 30-day period, amounting to +67%, +87%, and +285% above each group’s respective 12-month average. The longest-term holders, the 7y-10y and the 10y+, respectively, reached the 85th and 90th percentiles of transfer activity over the past four years.

Difficulty Rate Volatility Hits Highest Level Since 2021 China Mining Ban

Mining Dynamics: Hash Rate Drawdown Signals Bullish Setup

Over the past few months, hash rate and Bitcoin network mining difficulty dropped asymmetrically, with difficulty falling further than hash rate. While this may signal network churn, it is typically associated with above-average forward returns for bitcoin. The 30-day change in hash rate MA has declined to the 16th percentile over 30 days and 9th percentile over 90 days, while the change in difficulty MA is even weaker at the 5th and 6th percentiles, respectively. More striking is the clustering: 3 sustained hash rate decline episodes have occurred in just the past 5 months (December 2025, January-February 2026, March-April 2026). This is the densest concentration of drops since China’s mining ban in 2021.

In absolute terms, both metrics remain well below their recent peaks. The hash rate 30-day MA currently sits at 985.5 EH/s, down -7.5% from its all-time high of 1,065.7 EH/s set in late November 2025. Difficulty has declined -10.5% below its November 2025 peaks. Difficulty falling more than hash rate generally reflects the algorithm’s lagging adjustment mechanism and miner volatility. As marginal miners exited through 3 successive episodes, difficulty stepped down in discrete two-week resets and has not yet re-equilibrated to the recovering hash rate.

Encouragingly, the most recent episodes are shorter and shallower. The January-February 2026 episode lasted 31 days with a peak change in hash rate of -10.9%, while the March-April 2026 episode lasted just 16 days with a peak decline of -6.7%, ending on April 15, 2026. Across the 7 completed sustained decline episodes on record (excluding the 3 most recent, which lack sufficient forward data), the difficulty adjustment mechanism has acted as a stabilizer, giving surviving miners margin relief that encourages long-term hash rate growth.

In 6 of those 7 hash rate drawdown episodes, BTC price was higher 90 days after the episode ended, with a median gain of +37.7% (+2,000 bps uplift). Over 180 days, the median return was +63.1% (+2,190 bps uplift), ranging from -3.5% in June 2022 (the only loss in the dataset) to +199.3% in October 2020.

Stepping back, we have identified two strong bullish indicators based on historical data. Both mining rate drawdowns and negative funding rates have been associated with strong forward BTC returns. As such, we have become increasingly bullish on bitcoin.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Bitcoin funding rate, and why does it matter?

The Bitcoin funding rate is the periodic payment exchanged between long and short traders in perpetual futures contracts, reflecting whether bullish or bearish positioning dominates. Negative funding rates indicate short traders are paying longs, historically a contrarian signal. Since 2020, 30-day BTC returns during negative funding periods have averaged +11.5% versus +4.5% overall.

What does a drop in Bitcoin hash rate mean for the network and price?

A drop in hash rate typically indicates that marginal miners are turning off rigs as profitability compresses. While it may signal short-term network stress, historical data show that sustained hash rate drawdowns have been associated with above-average forward BTC returns. In 6 of 7 completed drawdown episodes since 2017, BTC was higher 90 days later, with a median gain of +37.7%.

Why do long-term Bitcoin holders’ spending patterns matter?

Spent volume by long-term holder cohorts can signal cyclical selling pressure or accumulation. Younger long-term holders (1y-5y) often correlate with 4-year cycle activity, while the longest-term cohorts (7y-10y, 10y+) rarely transact. When older cohorts increase spending, it can indicate profit-taking from some of the most seasoned bitcoin investors, though not all long-dormant movements represent selling.

Disclosures

Definitions

Bitcoin (BTC) is a decentralized digital currency without a central bank or single administrator. It can be sent from user to user on the peer-to-peer Bitcoin network without intermediaries.

Funding rate is the periodic payment exchanged between long and short positions in perpetual futures contracts, used to keep the contract price aligned with the underlying spot price.

Hash rate is the total computational power being used to mine and process transactions on the Bitcoin network, typically measured in exahashes per second (EH/s).

Digital Asset Treasuries (DATs) are publicly traded companies that hold significant portions of their balance sheet in digital assets such as Bitcoin, often using debt or equity issuance to fund purchases.

Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

Risk Considerations

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed.

Investments in digital assets and Web3 companies are highly speculative and involve a high degree of risk. These risks include, but are not limited to: the technology is new and many of its uses may be untested; intense competition; slow adoption rates and the potential for product obsolescence; volatility and limited liquidity, including but not limited to, inability to liquidate a position; loss or destruction of key(s) to access accounts or the blockchain; reliance on digital wallets; reliance on unregulated markets and exchanges; reliance on the internet; cybersecurity risks; and the lack of regulation and the potential for new laws and regulation that may be difficult to predict. Moreover, the extent to which Web3 companies or digital assets utilize blockchain technology may vary, and it is possible that even widespread adoption of blockchain technology may not result in a material increase in the value of such companies or digital assets.

Digital asset prices are highly volatile, and the value of digital assets, and Web3 companies, can rise or fall dramatically and quickly. If their value goes down, there’s no guarantee that it will rise again. As a result, there is a significant risk of loss of your entire principal investment.

Digital assets are not covered by FDIC or SIPC insurance.

Digital assets are digital representations of value that function as mediums of exchange, units of account, or stores of value, but they do not have legal tender status. Digital assets are sometimes exchanged for U.S. dollars or other currencies around the world, but they are generally not backed or supported by any government or central bank.

Web3 companies include but are not limited to, companies that involve the development, innovation, and/or utilization of blockchain, digital assets, or crypto technologies.
